summary:
The Kingdom of Kush was a powerful state centered in what is now northern Sudan, occupying the Nile valley south of Egypt. Its roots lay in the Kerma culture of the third millennium BC, but the fully organized kingdom emerged around 1070 BC following the withdrawal of Egyptian imperial control from Nubia at the end of the [[event:60|New Kingdom]].

For much of the second millennium BC, Nubia had been under Egyptian domination, with a series of fortress towns and administrative centers established to control gold mining, cattle, and the trade routes connecting sub-Saharan Africa to the Mediterranean world. Egyptian culture profoundly influenced Nubian elites, who adopted Egyptian religious practices, writing, and artistic styles even while preserving distinct local traditions. When Egyptian power retreated northward around 1070 BC, Kushite rulers at Napata consolidated an independent kingdom and began asserting their own imperial ambitions.

Kush reached the zenith of its power in the eighth century BC, when Kushite kings conquered Egypt and founded the Twenty-Fifth Dynasty (c. 747–656 BC), ruling a combined kingdom stretching from the confluence of the White and Blue Niles to the Mediterranean. The Kushite pharaohs portrayed themselves as restorers of traditional Egyptian religion and culture during a period of Egyptian weakness, sponsoring temple construction and reviving ancient ritual forms.

The Napatan phase of Kushite civilization was marked by distinctive pyramid tombs, temple complexes dedicated to the god Amun, and a royal succession system in which the king was selected by divine oracle from among eligible princes. Kush controlled valuable trade routes linking the Mediterranean world to sub-Saharan Africa, dealing in gold, ivory, ebony, and enslaved people. The kingdom represents one of the major independent African civilizations of antiquity, with a history extending down to the Meroitic period well into the first centuries AD.
